title: Multifractal Complexity of the Chandler Wobble and Its Anomalous Disappearance (2015--2020): A MFDFA Study
authors: Sebastián Jaroszewicz, Nahuel Mendez, Maria P. Beccar-Varela, Maria Cristina Mariani
abstract: The Chandler wobble (CW) -- the $\sim$433-day free nutation of Earth's rotation pole -- experienced an anomalous near-disappearance between 2015 and 2020, followed by a re-excitation with an approximately $180^{\circ}$ phase reversal. Using Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MFDFA) applied to more than six decades (1962--2024) of daily IERS EOP C04 polar motion data, this study provides the first multifractal characterisation of the CW and its recent anomaly. Global MFDFA shows that the residual polar motion components and the CW amplitude are genuine multifractal processes with strongly $q$-dependent generalised Hurst exponents and broad singularity spectra. Surrogate-data tests with shuffled and phase-randomised ensembles demonstrate that this multifractality originates from the combined action of long-range temporal correlations and heavy-tailed excitation statistics. A sliding-window analysis reveals a pronounced collapse in long-range persistence and multifractal spectral width of the geometric polar motion signal several years before and during the 2015--2020 amplitude minimum, indicating a genuine dynamical regime change rather than a simple suppression of oscillation amplitude. In contrast, the amplitude- and phase-related variables retain broad multifractal spectra and stable scaling exponents across all epochs, revealing a dynamical decoupling between the geometry of the CW and the multiscale structure of its amplitude and phase fluctuations. These findings highlight the CW amplitude as an exceptionally multifractal integrator of geophysical excitation and suggest that multifractal metrics may provide early-warning indicators of major transitions in Earth rotation dynamics.
summary: This work uses Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis on decades of Earth's polar motion data to study the Chandler Wobble. It finds that the CW's anomalous near-disappearance is marked by a measurable collapse in its multifractal complexity, suggesting a fundamental dynamical regime change. The study highlights that multifractal metrics can serve as potential early-warning indicators for major transitions in Earth's rotation dynamics.
main_problem: The study characterizes the multifractal complexity of the Chandler Wobble (CW) and investigates the physical nature of its anomalous near-disappearance between 2015 and 2020.
main_result: The CW amplitude dynamics show a collapse in long-range persistence and multifractal spectral width during the anomaly, indicating a genuine dynamical regime change, while the amplitude and phase fluctuations remain decoupled and highly complex.
method: Multifractal Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (MFDFA) is applied to polar motion data, supplemented by sliding-window analysis and rigorous surrogate-data testing to distinguish correlation sources.
model_or_system: The system is the Earth's rotation pole motion, specifically analyzing the Chandler Wobble (CW) component extracted from decades of daily IERS EOP C04 polar motion data.
key_observables: Generalized Hurst exponents h(q), multifractal spectral width (Δα), local persistence H(t), and the CW amplitude (ACW(t)).
important_parameters: The anomalous period (2015–2020), the CW period ($\sim 433$ days), and the time span (1962–2024).
main_assumptions: The multifractality originates from a combination of long-range temporal correlations and heavy-tailed excitation statistics; the decline in complexity signals a dynamical regime change rather than simple amplitude damping.
figures_summary: Figures show the non-linear scaling of h(q) confirming multifractality, and temporal plots illustrate the systematic collapse of H(t) and Δα(t) for geometric residuals during the anomalous period.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from global characterization of multifractality using MFDFA, to isolating the CW signal, and finally employs a sliding-window analysis to track the temporal evolution of complexity metrics across distinct geophysical regimes.
why_interesting: The application of multifractal analysis to geophysical time series provides a powerful tool for detecting non-equilibrium transitions and critical slowing-down signatures, which are concepts relevant to understanding complex dynamics in many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Step 1.2 — Decomposition of polar motion from the IERS EOP C04 series (1962–2024). Top row: raw xp (left) and yp (right) components in arcseconds. Middle row: detrended series (blue) after removal of the secular mean-pole drift, together with the isolated annual+semi-annual component obtained by narrow-band notch filtering (orange), for xp (left) and yp (right). Bottom row: residual polar motion xres p (left) and yres p (right), which contain predominantly the Chandler wobble plus broadband stochastic noise and are the starting point for the multifractal analysis.

Figure 2: Power spectral density (PSD) of the complex residual polar motion pres(t) = xres p + iyres p after removal of secular drift and annual/semi-annual signals. The dominant peak at f ≈0.843 cpy (T ≈433 days) corresponds to the Chandler wobble. Vertical dashed lines mark the bandpass filter limits (0.743–0.943 cpy) used for CW extraction.

Figure 3 presents ACW(t) and the unwrapped phase ϕCW(t) over the full record. The extracted amplitude time series ACW(t) exhibits several noteworthy dynamical fea- tures over the observational record. Following a broad maximum between approximately 1990 and 2010 with peak amplitudes reaching ∼200 mas, the signal underwent a progressive

Figure 4: Generalised Hurst exponent h(q) as a function of q for the four analysed series computed over the full 1962–2024 record. The clear non-linearity of h(q) and its strong q-dependence, especially for the amplitude series ACW and δACW, confirm the multifractal nature of the signals.

Figure 5: Global singularity spectra f(α) for the four analysed series computed from the full 1962–2024 record. The broad, non-parabolic shapes confirm genuine multifractality. The Chandler wobble amplitude ACW displays the largest spectral width ∆α, indicating the strongest degree of multifractality.
